seo.title: CSS Typography and Google Fonts - font-family, clamp, line-height, Google Fonts | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master CSS typography. Learn font-family with fallbacks, Google Fonts integration, font-size with clamp for fluid text, font-weight, line-height, letter-spacing, text-shadow, @font-face, and more. 55+ practice questions with complete runnable HTML examples.
seo.keywords: css typography, css font-family, google fonts tutorial, css clamp font-size, system font stack, line-height css, letter-spacing css, text-shadow css, @font-face css, web fonts, font-display swap, css font shorthand

content.what:
What Is Typography in CSS?

Typography is the art of arranging text to make it beautiful and readable. Good typography is the difference between a page that looks amateur and one that looks professional. The same content with different fonts, sizes, line heights, and spacing can feel like two completely different websites.

In this chapter you will learn every CSS property that affects text: the font itself, its size, weight, style, spacing, alignment, decoration, and transformation. You will also learn how to use Google Fonts (free, beautiful web fonts) in your own projects.

body {
  font-family: 'Inter', -apple-system, sans-serif;
  font-size: 16px;
  font-weight: 400;
  line-height: 1.6;
  letter-spacing: 0.01em;
  color: #1a1a2e;
}
content.why:
Why Does Typography Matter?

1. 90% of the Web Is Text

Most of what people read on the web is text. Blog posts, product descriptions, articles, forms. If your typography is bad, your users struggle to read and bounce. If your typography is great, they stay longer, understand better, and trust your site more.

2. Fonts Set the Entire Mood

Switch a site from Times New Roman to Inter and it feels modern. Switch to Playfair Display and it feels editorial. Switch to Fira Code and it feels technical. You cannot design a look without thinking about the font.

3. Google Fonts Is Free and Amazing

Google Fonts gives you thousands of professional-quality fonts for free. A single <link> tag in your HTML and you can use fonts like Poppins, Inter, Montserrat, Playfair Display, and many more. This chapter will show you exactly how.

4. Fluid Typography Is the Modern Way

Responsive design used to require media queries to change font sizes. Now you can write font-size: clamp(16px, 2vw, 24px) and the text automatically scales with the viewport. One line replaces multiple media queries.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1. font-family and Fallbacks

font-family specifies which font to use. You list multiple fonts as fallbacks in case the first one is not available. Always end with a generic family (serif, sans-serif, monospace).

body {
  font-family: 'Poppins', 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif;
}
code {
  font-family: 'Fira Code', 'Courier New', monospace;
}
h1 {
  font-family: 'Playfair Display', Georgia, serif;
}

Rule: fonts with a space in the name must be quoted ('Fira Code'). Single-word fonts do not need quotes (Arial).

2. System Font Stack

The system font stack uses whatever font the user's operating system uses for its interface. It loads instantly (no download) and looks native on every platform.

body {
  font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Roboto, 'Helvetica Neue', Arial, sans-serif;
}

This gives you San Francisco on macOS/iOS, Segoe UI on Windows, Roboto on Android, and fallbacks for everything else.

3. Google Fonts

Visit fonts.google.com, pick a font, copy the <link> tag, paste it in your HTML head, then use the font-family name.

<!-- In HTML head -->
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>
<link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Poppins:wght@400;600;800&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">

<!-- In CSS -->
body { font-family: 'Poppins', sans-serif; }
h1 { font-weight: 800; }

The wght@400;600;800 part tells Google which weights to download. Only request the ones you use -- fewer weights means faster page load.

4. font-size Units

  • px: absolute pixels. font-size: 16px. Fixed and predictable.
  • em: relative to parent font-size. 1.5em = 1.5x parent. Compounds if nested.
  • rem: relative to root (html) font-size. 1.5rem = 1.5x root. Does not compound. Preferred for most sizing.
  • %: relative to parent font-size (like em).
  • vw: viewport width. 5vw = 5% of browser width. Scales with screen.
  • clamp(min, preferred, max): fluid typography that scales between a minimum and maximum.
h1 {
  font-size: clamp(24px, 4vw, 48px);
  /* At least 24px, grows with viewport width, caps at 48px */
}

5. font-weight

Weight values: 100 (thin) to 900 (black). Or keywords: normal (400), bold (700), lighter, bolder.

h1 { font-weight: 800; }    /* Extra bold */
p  { font-weight: 400; }    /* Normal */
em { font-weight: 500; }    /* Medium */

Only weights you loaded in Google Fonts are available. If you load 400 and 700, asking for 600 will fall back to the closest one.

6. font-style

Values: normal, italic, oblique.

em { font-style: italic; }
.quote { font-style: italic; color: #6b7280; }

7. line-height

Vertical space between lines. Use unitless values like 1.5 or 1.6 instead of px. Unitless values multiply by the element's font-size, and children inherit the multiplier (not the computed value) -- which behaves correctly.

body { line-height: 1.6; }          /* GOOD */
body { line-height: 24px; }         /* Bad -- does not scale with font-size */

h1 { line-height: 1.2; }            /* Tight for headings */
p  { line-height: 1.6; }            /* Airy for paragraphs */

8. letter-spacing and word-spacing

Adjust the space between letters and words. Negative values tighten, positive values loosen.

h1 { letter-spacing: -0.02em; } /* Slightly tighter */
.small-caps {
  text-transform: uppercase;
  letter-spacing: 0.1em;          /* Loose uppercase looks elegant */
  font-size: 12px;
}

p { word-spacing: 2px; }

9. text-align

Values: left (default), right, center, justify.

h1 { text-align: center; }
.quote { text-align: right; }

10. text-decoration

Controls underlines, strikethroughs, and overlines. Common on links.

a { text-decoration: none; }                          /* remove underline */
a:hover { text-decoration: underline; }
del { text-decoration: line-through; }
.fancy { text-decoration: underline wavy #a855f7; }   /* modern shorthand */

11. text-transform

Changes letter case visually (HTML stays the same).

.upper { text-transform: uppercase; }
.lower { text-transform: lowercase; }
.cap   { text-transform: capitalize; }

12. text-shadow

Adds a shadow behind text. Syntax: x-offset y-offset blur color.

h1 { text-shadow: 0 2px 10px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3); }

/* Multiple shadows create glows and 3D effects */
h1 {
  text-shadow:
    0 0 10px #a855f7,
    0 0 20px #a855f7,
    0 0 40px #a855f7;
}

13. @font-face for Custom Fonts

If you have a custom font file, use @font-face to declare it, then use it like any other font.

@font-face {
  font-family: 'MyCustomFont';
  src: url('fonts/mycustom.woff2') format('woff2'),
       url('fonts/mycustom.woff') format('woff');
  font-weight: 400;
  font-style: normal;
  font-display: swap; /* Show fallback while loading */
}

body { font-family: 'MyCustomFont', sans-serif; }

14. font-display: swap

font-display: swap tells the browser: show the fallback font immediately, then swap to the web font when it loads. This prevents invisible text during font loading (known as FOIT). Always include it.

15. Web-Safe Fonts

Fonts installed on almost every device. Safe to use without loading anything: Arial, Georgia, Times New Roman, Courier New, Verdana, Tahoma, Trebuchet MS, Impact. Use them as fallbacks or when you need zero load time.

codeExamples.title: Google Fonts with Poppins
codeExamples.code:
Modern Typography

A guide by Aarav Patel

Why fonts matter

Choosing the right font gives your content a voice. Poppins is a modern geometric sans-serif that feels clean and professional. It works beautifully for headings, buttons, and body text.

Weights available

This page loads four weights: 300 light, 400 normal, 600 semibold, and 800 extra bold. Each weight expresses a different mood.
codeExamples.explanation: Google Fonts are loaded with a <link> tag in the HTML head. The wght@300;400;600;800 parameter requests four specific weights. display=swap ensures text is visible during load. In CSS, just reference 'Poppins' as a normal font-family with a fallback stack.
codeExamples.output: A page with a bold Poppins heading, a light gray subtitle, purple section headers, and well-spaced body text.

codeExamples.title: Fluid Typography with clamp()
codeExamples.code:
Resize your browser

Fluid typography example by Priya Sharma

All headings and paragraphs on this page use clamp() to scale fluidly. There is no media query. As you make the window wider, the text grows smoothly between its minimum and maximum.

How it works

clamp(min, preferred, max) takes three values: the smallest size allowed, the desired size (usually a viewport unit), and the largest size. The browser picks whichever is valid within the range.
codeExamples.explanation: clamp(32px, 6vw, 72px) means: never smaller than 32px, never larger than 72px, and at sizes in between, use 6% of the viewport width. Resize the browser to see the text scale smoothly. No media queries needed.
codeExamples.output: A dark page where headings and paragraphs smoothly scale as you resize the browser window.

codeExamples.title: Beautiful Blog Post Typography
codeExamples.code:
Learning to Code at 13

By Kavya Reddy - April 11, 2026

When I started coding, I had no idea what a variable was. My teacher Mr Verma told me just to type along and it would start making sense. He was right.

The best way to learn to code is to build things you care about.

Two years later, I have built a snake game, a to-do app, and my own website. If you are just starting, pick a font like Merriweather for your blog and your posts will look instantly more professional.

The tricks are simple: use a serif font, keep paragraph width around 60-70 characters, set line-height to 1.6 or higher, and pick a strong sans-serif for headings.
codeExamples.explanation: This uses two Google Fonts: Merriweather (serif) for body text and Inter (sans-serif) for headings. max-width: 680px limits line length for comfortable reading. line-height: 1.75 gives airy spacing. A blockquote with a colored left border is a classic blog pattern.
codeExamples.output: A polished blog post layout with a bold Inter heading, italic blockquote, and easy-to-read serif body text.

codeExamples.title: Text Shadow Glow Effect
codeExamples.code:
RIYA

Codes After Midnight

A simple soft glow for comparison.
codeExamples.explanation: Multiple text-shadows create a neon glow effect. Each shadow adds a larger, softer glow on top of the previous one. The innermost shadow is white to brighten the letters, and outer shadows are purple for the halo.
codeExamples.output: A dark page with a glowing neon purple 'RIYA' title and a cyan subtitle, all with dramatic shadow effects.

codeExamples.title: Monospace Code Block with Fira Code
codeExamples.code:
A snippet from Ishaan's first program

Declare a variable with let and print it:

// First program
let name = 'Ishaan';
console.log('Hello, ' + name);
codeExamples.explanation: Fira Code is a monospace font designed for code with programming ligatures. It is loaded from Google Fonts. The pre block preserves whitespace and uses the monospace font. Inline code tags use the same font with a light background.
codeExamples.output: A page with a dark code block showing a JavaScript snippet in Fira Code font with purple keywords, green strings, and gray comments.

codeExamples.title: Font Shorthand and Text Transforms
codeExamples.code:
LIMITED OFFER

Frontend Masterclass

Rs 2,999 Rs 4,999

"This course changed the way I think about design." - Meera Nair
codeExamples.explanation: The font shorthand combines style, weight, size, line-height, and family in one line: font: italic bold 20px/1.3 Georgia, serif;. The eyebrow label uses text-transform and letter-spacing to create a polished uppercase look. text-decoration: line-through creates the crossed-out original price.
codeExamples.output: Two cards: a product card with an uppercase eyebrow, italic title, and strike-through original price; and a testimonial card with an italic quote and colored left border.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting Quotes Around Font Names with Spaces
commonMistakes.wrongCode: body { font-family: Times New Roman, serif; }
commonMistakes.error: The browser tries to parse 'Times', 'New', and 'Roman' as three separate font names, failing.
commonMistakes.explanation: Any font name with spaces must be wrapped in quotes. Single-word fonts like Arial or Georgia do not need quotes, but it is fine (and consistent) to always quote them.

commonMistakes.title: Using px for line-height
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
body { font-size: 16px; line-height: 24px; }
h1 { font-size: 32px; /* line-height is STILL 24px -- too tight! */ }
commonMistakes.error: Children inherit the fixed 24px line-height. A 32px heading with 24px line-height overlaps badly.
commonMistakes.correctCode:
body { font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.5; }
h1 { font-size: 32px; /* line-height is now 32 * 1.5 = 48px */ }
commonMistakes.explanation: Unitless line-height multiplies by the element's own font-size. Fixed px line-height breaks when children have different font sizes. Always use unitless line-height like 1.5 or 1.6.

commonMistakes.title: Loading Too Many Google Font Weights
commonMistakes.error: This loads 9 font weights. Each weight is a separate file download, slowing page load significantly.
commonMistakes.explanation: Only load the weights you actually use. Most designs need 2-3 weights. Loading all 9 can add 500KB+ to your page for no visual benefit.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting font-display: swap
commonMistakes.error: Without display=swap, text is invisible until the font downloads (FOIT - Flash of Invisible Text). Users see blank content for up to 3 seconds.
commonMistakes.explanation: Always include &display=swap. This tells the browser to show the fallback font immediately, then swap to the web font when it loads. Much better user experience.

summary: font-family lists fonts as fallbacks, ending with a generic family (serif, sans-serif, monospace). Quote any font name containing spaces: 'Times New Roman'.
System font stack (-apple-system, Segoe UI, Roboto) uses the OS default font. Instant load, native look on every platform. Great default choice.
Google Fonts is free and easy: add a link tag in HTML head, use the font-family name in CSS. Always include display=swap to prevent invisible text during load.
font-size units: px (absolute), em (relative to parent), rem (relative to root -- preferred), vw (viewport width), clamp() (fluid). Use rem for consistent sizing.
clamp(min, preferred, max) creates fluid typography: clamp(24px, 4vw, 48px) scales between 24 and 48 as the viewport changes. Replaces media queries.
font-weight goes from 100 (thin) to 900 (black). Only weights you loaded are available. Keywords: normal = 400, bold = 700.
line-height should be unitless (1.5, 1.6). Unitless values scale correctly when children have different font sizes. Never use px for line-height on parent elements.
letter-spacing tightens or loosens character spacing. Negative values (-0.02em) for big headings. Positive values (0.1em) for uppercase labels.
text-transform changes case visually: uppercase, lowercase, capitalize. The HTML source stays the same; only display changes.
text-shadow syntax: x-offset y-offset blur color. Stack multiple shadows for neon glows, 3D effects, and drop shadows. @font-face loads custom fonts; always include font-display: swap.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this declare?
font-family: 'Poppins', Arial, sans-serif;
topicSpecific.answer: Try Poppins first. If not available, use Arial. If Arial is also missing, use the default sans-serif font.
topicSpecific.explanation: Font-family uses a fallback chain. The browser tries each font left to right and uses the first one it finds.

topicSpecific.question: Why must 'Times New Roman' be in quotes?
topicSpecific.answer: Because it contains spaces. Font names with spaces must be quoted so the parser treats them as a single name.
topicSpecific.explanation: Without quotes, the CSS parser sees 'Times', 'New', and 'Roman' as three different fonts.

topicSpecific.question: What is the default font-weight for normal?
topicSpecific.hint: Weights run 100 to 900 in steps of 100.
topicSpecific.explanation: font-weight: normal is equivalent to 400. bold is 700. Extra-bold is 800. Thin is 100.

topicSpecific.question: What is the unit-free line-height of 1.6 relative to?
topicSpecific.hint: Unitless line-heights multiply the element's font-size.
topicSpecific.answer: The element's own font-size. A 20px font with line-height 1.6 gets a 32px line-height.
topicSpecific.explanation: Unitless line-heights are multipliers. Each element calculates its own line-height based on its own font-size. This behaves correctly when children have different sizes.

topicSpecific.question: What does text-transform: uppercase do?
topicSpecific.answer: Displays the text in all capital letters, without changing the actual HTML.
topicSpecific.explanation: text-transform is visual only. The underlying text in the HTML stays unchanged. Useful for labels, buttons, and headings.

topicSpecific.question: What does font-size: 2rem produce if the root font-size is 16px?
topicSpecific.answer: 32px.
topicSpecific.explanation: rem is relative to the root (html) element's font-size. 2rem means 2 times the root size. 2 x 16 = 32.

topicSpecific.question: What does text-decoration: none do?
topicSpecific.hint: Think about what links look like by default.
topicSpecific.answer: Removes the underline from links (or the line from <del> tags).
topicSpecific.explanation: By default links have text-decoration: underline. Setting it to none removes the underline. A common reset for navigation menus.

topicSpecific.question: What three values does text-shadow need at minimum?
topicSpecific.answer: x-offset, y-offset, and color. Blur is optional.
topicSpecific.explanation: Minimal text-shadow: text-shadow: 2px 2px red;. Full form with blur: text-shadow: 2px 2px 8px red;.

topicSpecific.question: What does letter-spacing: 0.1em do?
topicSpecific.answer: Adds space equal to 10% of the font size between every character.
topicSpecific.explanation: Positive letter-spacing loosens character spacing. 0.1em at 20px font-size = 2px between letters. Commonly used on uppercase labels.

topicSpecific.question: Which is a monospace font?
topicSpecific.answer: Fira Code, Courier New, and Monaco are monospace fonts. Every character has the same width.
topicSpecific.explanation: Monospace fonts are used for code because each character takes equal horizontal space, aligning columns cleanly.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the result?
font-size: clamp(16px, 3vw, 24px);
topicSpecific.answer: The font-size is at least 16px, grows to 3% of the viewport width at medium sizes, and never exceeds 24px.
topicSpecific.explanation: clamp(min, preferred, max) keeps the value between min and max, using the preferred value (which can be a dynamic unit like vw) in between.

topicSpecific.question: What is the font-family of system-ui?
topicSpecific.hint: It is a generic family like sans-serif.
topicSpecific.answer: Whatever font the user's operating system uses for its UI (San Francisco on macOS, Segoe UI on Windows, Roboto on Android).
topicSpecific.explanation: system-ui tells the browser to use the native system font. Instant load, matches platform look. Supported in modern browsers.

topicSpecific.question: Why is unitless line-height better than px for a parent element?
topicSpecific.hint: Think about a h1 with bigger font inside.
topicSpecific.answer: Unitless line-heights multiply the child's own font-size, so headings and paragraphs each get proportional spacing. Fixed px line-heights inherit as-is and may be too tight for large children.
topicSpecific.explanation: If body has line-height: 24px and h1 is 40px, the heading's lines will overlap. Unitless (1.5) gives each element its own proportional line-height.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this shorthand set?
font: italic bold 18px/1.5 'Poppins', sans-serif;
topicSpecific.answer: font-style: italic, font-weight: bold, font-size: 18px, line-height: 1.5, font-family: 'Poppins', sans-serif.
topicSpecific.explanation: The font shorthand combines up to 5 properties. Size/line-height is separated by a slash. Family must come last.

topicSpecific.question: What does &display=swap do in a Google Fonts URL?
topicSpecific.hint: Think about what happens while the font is loading.
topicSpecific.answer: Tells the browser to show the fallback font immediately, then swap to the web font once it finishes downloading.
topicSpecific.explanation: Without swap, browsers may hide text until the font loads (Flash of Invisible Text). swap prevents this by showing fallback text right away.

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between em and rem?
topicSpecific.hint: What are they each relative to?
topicSpecific.answer: em is relative to the parent element's font-size (compounds when nested). rem is relative to the root (html) font-size (no compounding).
topicSpecific.explanation: Nested em values multiply: if parent is 2em and child is 1.5em, the child is 3x root. rem always references the root, avoiding surprises.

topicSpecific.question: What happens if the browser cannot load a Google Font?
topicSpecific.answer: The browser uses the next font in the font-family fallback list (Arial, Georgia, sans-serif, etc.).
topicSpecific.explanation: The fallback chain is the safety net. If the Google Font fails to load, the page still renders with a backup font rather than showing unstyled text.

topicSpecific.question: What does text-decoration: underline wavy #a855f7 do?
topicSpecific.hint: Modern shorthand allows style and color.
topicSpecific.answer: Applies a wavy purple underline under the text.
topicSpecific.explanation: Modern text-decoration accepts three parts: line (underline/overline/line-through), style (solid/wavy/dotted/dashed), and color.

topicSpecific.question: Which text-align value justifies text to both left and right margins?
topicSpecific.answer: justify
topicSpecific.explanation: text-align: justify adjusts word spacing so text lines up on both sides. Common in print, less common online due to uneven gaps.

topicSpecific.question: What does font-weight: 800 mean?
topicSpecific.answer: Extra bold (heavier than regular bold which is 700).
topicSpecific.explanation: 800 is extra bold. Only works if that weight was loaded for the font. Otherwise, the browser uses the nearest available weight.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the computed line-height in px?
body { font-size: 18px; line-height: 1.6; }
topicSpecific.explanation: Unitless line-height multiplies the font-size. 18 * 1.6 = 28.8px. Browsers may round to 29px when rendering.

topicSpecific.question:
Why does this cause a problem?
@font-face { font-family: 'MyFont'; src: url('font.woff2') format('woff2'); }
/* No font-display */
topicSpecific.hint: Think about what the user sees while the font is downloading.
topicSpecific.answer: The browser hides text until the font loads (FOIT - Flash of Invisible Text), causing blank content for up to 3 seconds.
topicSpecific.explanation: Always include font-display: swap in @font-face to show fallback text immediately while the custom font loads.

topicSpecific.hint: body is 1rem (relative to root), h1 is 2em (relative to body).
topicSpecific.explanation: rem is always relative to root. em is relative to the parent. body computes to 18px. h1 is 2em relative to body = 36px.

topicSpecific.question: When should you pick a system font stack over Google Fonts?
topicSpecific.hint: Think load time and design intent.
topicSpecific.answer: Use system fonts when performance is critical (no download), when you want a native platform look, or when the design doesn't need a custom personality. Use Google Fonts when you want a specific brand look or a font that is consistent across all devices.
topicSpecific.explanation: System fonts load instantly with zero bandwidth. Google Fonts give you brand consistency. Many sites use system fonts for body text and Google Fonts for headings to balance speed and style.

topicSpecific.question: Why use max-width: 60ch or max-width: 65ch for paragraph text?
topicSpecific.hint: ch = width of the '0' character. Think readability studies.
topicSpecific.answer: Typography research shows paragraphs are easiest to read when lines contain 45-75 characters. max-width: 65ch limits line length to roughly 65 characters, improving reading comfort and reducing eye fatigue.
topicSpecific.explanation: The ch unit is based on character width. Using it for max-width keeps paragraphs in the ideal readability range regardless of font-size.

mixed.question: What is font-weight: bold equal to numerically?
mixed.explanation: The keyword bold corresponds to the numeric weight 700. normal = 400.

mixed.question: Which unit is relative to the viewport width?
mixed.explanation: 1vw = 1% of the viewport width. 100vw is the full viewport width. Useful for fluid typography and full-width layouts.

mixed.question: What does text-transform: capitalize do?
mixed.answer: Capitalizes the first letter of each word.
mixed.explanation: capitalize only affects the first letter of each word. Use uppercase for all caps, lowercase for all lowercase.

mixed.question: What does text-decoration: line-through look like?
mixed.answer: Text with a horizontal line through the middle, like crossed-out text.
mixed.explanation: Used for strikethrough effects, old prices, or deleted content. The <del> HTML element has this style by default.

mixed.question: Which generic font family is always monospace?
mixed.explanation: The generic families are serif, sans-serif, monospace, cursive, and fantasy. monospace guarantees a fixed-width font.

mixed.question: What is the advantage of rem over px?
mixed.hint: Think accessibility - users changing the default font-size.
mixed.answer: rem scales with the root font-size. If a user sets a larger default font-size in their browser for accessibility, rem values scale up too. px stays fixed.
mixed.explanation: Using rem respects user font-size preferences. Many accessibility guidelines recommend rem over px for font-size.

mixed.question:
What does this shorthand NOT include?
font: 18px/1.5 Arial;
mixed.hint: Minimal form with just size, line-height, and family.
mixed.answer: font-style and font-weight are not specified. They will use their default values (normal, normal).
mixed.explanation: The font shorthand resets any omitted properties to their defaults. If you only want to change size and family, use longhand to preserve other settings.

mixed.question: How do you make link text NOT underlined?
mixed.explanation: Browsers apply text-decoration: underline to links by default. Set it to none to remove the underline. Add your own hover style if needed.

mixed.question: How do you make fallback text visible while a web font loads?
mixed.answer: Use font-display: swap in @font-face or &display=swap in the Google Fonts URL.
mixed.explanation: swap tells browsers to show fallback text immediately and swap to the web font once it downloads. Without it, users may see blank content.

mixed.question:
What does this do?
text-shadow: 2px 2px 0 black, 4px 4px 0 #a855f7;
mixed.answer: Creates a 3D/layered effect with a black shadow 2px down-right and a purple shadow 4px further down-right.
mixed.explanation: Multiple text-shadows are painted in order (first on top). Offsets create depth. This pattern is popular for retro/poster-style text.

mixed.question: What does font-style: italic do?
mixed.answer: Displays text in the italic variant of the font (slanted characters).
mixed.explanation: Requires the font to have an italic style. If not, the browser may synthesize a slanted version which usually looks worse.

mixed.question: Why load only the font weights you need from Google Fonts?
mixed.hint: Each weight is a separate file.
mixed.answer: Each weight is a separate font file that the browser must download. Loading 9 weights instead of 3 can add hundreds of kilobytes and slow page load noticeably.
mixed.explanation: Most designs need 2-4 weights (for example 400, 600, 800). Load only those. Faster pages and better Lighthouse scores.

mixed.question: What is a good line-height for body paragraphs?
mixed.answer: Unitless 1.5 to 1.8. Lower (1.2) for headings, higher for body paragraphs.
mixed.explanation: Airy line-heights improve readability for long text. Headings look better with tight line-heights because they are short and large.

mixed.question: Which is a web-safe font available on almost every device?
mixed.answer: Arial, Georgia, Times New Roman, Courier New, Verdana, Tahoma, Trebuchet MS, and Impact are widely available.
mixed.explanation: Web-safe fonts are preinstalled on nearly every operating system. You can use them without loading anything, making them ideal for fallbacks.

mixed.question: Why would you combine a serif font for body and a sans-serif for headings?
mixed.answer: Serif fonts have decorative strokes that improve readability in long paragraphs. Sans-serif fonts look clean and modern for headings. Combining them creates visual contrast and hierarchy.
mixed.explanation: Example pairing: Merriweather (serif body) + Inter (sans-serif headings). The serif makes reading paragraphs comfortable; the sans-serif gives headings a crisp modern look.

mixed.question:
What is the result?
p::first-line { font-weight: bold; }
mixed.answer: Makes only the first line of every paragraph bold.
mixed.explanation: ::first-line selects the rendered first line of a block element. The bold effect stops when the text wraps to the next line. Common in editorial designs.

mixed.question:
What is the effect?
h1 { font: 600 clamp(32px, 5vw, 72px)/1.1 'Inter', sans-serif; letter-spacing: -0.02em; }
mixed.hint: Shorthand plus clamp plus tighter letter-spacing.
mixed.answer: A semibold h1 in Inter with fluid font-size (32-72px), tight line-height (1.1), and slightly tightened letter-spacing for a polished modern heading.
mixed.explanation: This is a production-ready heading recipe. clamp for fluidity, tight line-height for headings, slightly negative letter-spacing for big text.

mixed.question: What is the difference between FOIT and FOUT?
mixed.hint: Both are font loading issues. One flashes invisible, one flashes unstyled.
mixed.answer: FOIT (Flash of Invisible Text) is when the browser hides text completely while the font loads. FOUT (Flash of Unstyled Text) is when the browser shows fallback text first then swaps to the web font. font-display: swap causes FOUT, which is usually preferred.
mixed.explanation: FOUT is a brief visual shift but the user can read immediately. FOIT hides content completely. Most designers prefer FOUT because content is accessible sooner.

mixed.question: How does ch unit work?
mixed.hint: It is based on a character.
mixed.answer: The ch unit equals the width of the '0' (zero) character in the current font. max-width: 60ch limits a container to about 60 characters wide, regardless of font-size.
mixed.explanation: ch is perfect for typography because it scales naturally with the font. Researchers recommend 45-75 characters per line for best readability, so 60-65ch is a safe default.

mixed.question: Why add <link rel="preconnect"> before the Google Fonts link?
mixed.answer: preconnect tells the browser to start establishing a connection to fonts.googleapis.com and fonts.gstatic.com early, before it actually needs the font. This shaves milliseconds off font loading time.
mixed.explanation: Google Fonts recommends adding two preconnect links before the stylesheet link. It is a small but measurable performance improvement.

mcqs.question: Which CSS property sets the font?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. font-family sets the typeface. It accepts a list of fallback fonts ending with a generic family.

mcqs.question: Which unit is relative to the ROOT font-size?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. rem = 'root em'. Relative to the html element's font-size. em is relative to the parent.

mcqs.question: What is font-weight: bold numerically?
mcqs.explanation: D is correct. The keyword bold corresponds to numeric weight 700. normal is 400.

mcqs.question: Which line-height is correct?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Unitless line-heights scale correctly with the element's own font-size. Preferred for almost all cases.

mcqs.question: Which removes the underline from a link?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. text-decoration: none removes underlines, overlines, and line-throughs.

mcqs.question: How do you display text in all uppercase without changing the HTML?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. text-transform: uppercase renders the text in caps visually without modifying the source.

mcqs.question: Which generic font family is for code?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. monospace guarantees every character has the same width, which is ideal for code.

mcqs.question: How do you center text horizontally?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. text-align: center centers inline content within its block container.

mcqs.question: Which is a web-safe font?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Arial is preinstalled on virtually every device. The others are web fonts that require loading.

mcqs.question: What does &display=swap do?

options.B: Shows fallback text until the web font loads, then swaps to the web font

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. swap keeps text visible during font loading by showing fallback first, then swapping to the web font.

mcqs.question: What does clamp(16px, 2vw, 24px) return?

options.C: A value between 16px and 24px based on viewport width

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. clamp(min, preferred, max) returns the preferred value (2vw), but clamped to never go below 16px or above 24px.

mcqs.question: Which font property quoting is correct?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Names with spaces must be quoted. Multiple fonts are separated by commas, ending with a generic family.

mcqs.question: What is the font shorthand order?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The order is: font-style font-variant font-weight font-size/line-height font-family. Family must be last.

mcqs.question: Which is better for accessibility: px or rem for font-size?

options.B: rem (it respects user's default font-size setting)

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. rem scales with the root font-size, which users can increase for accessibility. px stays fixed regardless.

mcqs.question: Which text-shadow creates a soft glow?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Zero offsets with a large blur produce a soft glow around the text.

mcqs.question: What is a reasonable max-width for comfortable reading?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. 45-75 characters per line is the sweet spot. 65ch or around 680px work well for body text.

mcqs.question: Why add preconnect links before Google Fonts?

options.B: Faster font loading by establishing connections early

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. preconnect warms up the DNS and TLS handshake before the browser needs the font file, reducing load time.

mcqs.question: Which declares a custom font from your own file?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. @font-face declares a custom font with its file location, format, weight, style, and display strategy.

mcqs.question: What does letter-spacing: -0.02em do to big headings?

options.B: Tightens characters slightly, which looks better for large display text

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Big headings look better with slightly tightened letter-spacing. Positive spacing is for small uppercase labels.

mcqs.question: Which is NOT a valid font-weight value?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. 'heavy' is not a CSS keyword. Valid keywords: normal, bold, bolder, lighter. Numeric: 100-900.

mcqs.question: What is the FOIT problem with web fonts?

options.A: The font file is too big
options.B: Text is invisible while the web font downloads

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Flash of Invisible Text: the browser hides text until the web font loads. font-display: swap fixes this by showing fallback text immediately.

mcqs.question: Why use unitless line-height on the body element?

options.B: Unitless values multiply the element's own font-size, so headings and paragraphs get proportional spacing
options.C: It is the only way it works

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Unitless line-heights scale per-element. A fixed px line-height on body would be inherited as-is and cause overlapping lines on big headings.

mcqs.question: Which combination creates clean modern blog typography?

options.B: Merriweather serif body, Inter sans-serif headings, line-height 1.7, max-width 65ch
options.C: Arial bold everywhere, justify align, single line

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. A serif for body improves reading comfort. Sans-serif for headings adds contrast. Airy line-height and a sensible max-width round out the recipe.

mcqs.question: Which is the best way to include Google Fonts for performance?

options.A: Load all 9 weights you might ever need
options.B: Load only the 2-3 weights you actually use with display=swap and preconnect
options.C: Download the font files and host them yourself always

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Minimum weights, swap for visible fallback, preconnect to warm up the connection. @import blocks rendering and is slower than a link tag.

mcqs.question: What is the difference between em and rem when font-size changes?

options.B: em compounds through nested elements; rem always references the root
options.C: em is pixel-based; rem is percent-based

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. em is relative to the parent and compounds: 1.5em inside 1.5em = 2.25x root. rem always references the html element.

codingChallenges.title: Import and Apply Poppins
codingChallenges.description: Create a page with a heading 'Hello from Priya' and a short paragraph. Import Poppins from Google Fonts with weights 400 and 800. Apply Poppins to the whole body. Heading should be weight 800, paragraph weight 400.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A page with a bold Poppins heading and a regular Poppins paragraph.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use preconnect, display=swap, and load only the needed weights.
codingChallenges.solution:
Hello from Priya

This page uses the Poppins font loaded from Google Fonts. The heading is weight 800, the paragraph is weight 400.

codingChallenges.title: Fluid Heading with clamp
codingChallenges.description: Create a page with a heading that uses clamp() to scale smoothly from 32px (minimum) to 64px (maximum) based on viewport width. Resize the window to test.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: The heading grows and shrinks smoothly as the window changes width.
codingChallenges.solution:
Resize me, Aarav!

This heading scales smoothly between 32px and 64px as the window width changes. No media queries.

codingChallenges.title: Blog Post Layout
codingChallenges.description: Build a blog post with Merriweather serif for body and Inter sans-serif for headings. Include a title, meta line (author + date in uppercase tracked label style), 2 paragraphs, and an italic blockquote with colored left border.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A clean blog post with serif body, sans-serif title, uppercase meta, and a styled blockquote.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use two Google Fonts. max-width 680px, line-height 1.75, letter-spacing on meta line.
codingChallenges.solution:
Why I Love Coding

By Kavya Reddy - April 11, 2026

Coding gives me a superpower. I can take an idea from my head and turn it into something I can click, play with, and share with my friends. That feeling never gets old.

The only way to learn a new programming language is by writing programs in it.

Last week I built a calculator app, and today I am learning typography. Every day is a new adventure.

codingChallenges.title: Neon Text Effect
codingChallenges.description: Create a dark background page with the text 'RIYA CODES' as a big heading that glows neon purple using multiple text-shadows. Add a subtitle below it with a softer cyan glow.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Big neon purple 'RIYA CODES' glowing on a dark background with a cyan subtitle.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use multiple stacked text-shadows. Dark body background. Center everything.
codingChallenges.solution:
RIYA CODES

Until The Sun Rises

codingChallenges.title: Product Card Typography
codingChallenges.description: Build a product card with an uppercase amber 'LIMITED OFFER' eyebrow label (tracked letter-spacing), a bold heading, a price with a line-through original price next to it in gray, and a description paragraph. Use proper type hierarchy.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A card showing all elements with clear typographic hierarchy.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use the font shorthand. text-transform and letter-spacing on the eyebrow. text-decoration line-through on original price.
codingChallenges.solution:
LIMITED OFFER

Frontend Masterclass

Rs 2,999Rs 4,999

A 12-week course for teens by Ishaan. Learn HTML, CSS, and JavaScript by building real projects.

codingChallenges.title: Typography System Page
codingChallenges.description: Build a 'Typography System' demo page that shows H1, H2, H3, paragraph, blockquote, ordered list, and code elements with consistent professional styling. Use one sans-serif Google Font.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A design-system style documentation page showing every text element with clear hierarchy.
codingChallenges.constraints: All sizes use rem. Unitless line-heights. max-width 700px. Clean spacing between elements.
codingChallenges.solution:
Typography System

A consistent type scale for everything Meera builds.

Heading 2: Section Title

This is a paragraph of body text. Good typography feels effortless to read. The line-height is 1.6 and the max-width keeps lines at a comfortable length.

Heading 3: Subsection

Use font-family with fallbacks and line-height as an unitless value for best results.

"Typography is two-dimensional architecture." - Hermann Zapf

Ordered list

  1. Pick one sans-serif font for the UI.
  2. Use 2-3 weights (400, 600, 800).
  3. Set unitless line-height on the body.
  4. Limit line length to 65ch for readability.
